title: Who Is This Agent? Machine Identity Security

modules.title: Defining Machine Identity: How AI Agents Authenticate and Act on Your Behalf
modules.sub: Mapping how agents establish, prove, and exercise their identities
modules.description: Learners examine the authentication mechanisms AI agents use — from API keys and service accounts to certificates and OAuth tokens — and compare them against traditional human identity models. They identify where conventional IAM frameworks fall short when applied to autonomous, non-human principals. By the end, learners can articulate the unique identity surface area introduced by AI agent deployments in their own environments.

modules.title: Credential and Token Management for Autonomous AI Workflows
modules.sub: Securing secrets and tokens throughout the autonomous agent lifecycle
modules.description: Learners evaluate common credential management pitfalls specific to AI workflows, including hardcoded secrets, long-lived tokens, and over-permissioned service accounts that agents inherit. They apply best practices such as dynamic secret injection, short-lived token rotation, and secrets-manager integration to representative agent architectures. The module concludes with learners producing a credential hygiene checklist tailored to autonomous AI pipelines.

modules.title: Privilege Escalation and Lateral Movement via Compromised Agent Identities
modules.sub: Recognizing and containing agent identity exploitation attack paths
modules.description: Learners step through realistic attack scenarios in which a compromised agent identity is used to escalate privileges or pivot laterally across systems, APIs, and cloud resources. They analyze the specific properties of agent identities — persistence, automation speed, and broad scoping — that make them attractive targets and force multipliers for adversaries. Learners then map defensive chokepoints and prioritize mitigations that break the kill chain at the identity layer.

modules.title: Implementing Zero-Trust Principles for Non-Human Principals
modules.sub: Applying least-privilege and continuous verification to AI agents
modules.description: Learners translate core zero-trust tenets — never trust, always verify, enforce least privilege — into concrete policy configurations for non-human identities such as AI agents, bots, and service workloads. They design access policies that scope agent permissions to the minimum required action, incorporate just-in-time access grants, and enforce continuous re-verification rather than static trust. The module challenges learners to adapt an existing agent deployment to meet a zero-trust policy baseline they define.

modules.title: Auditing and Monitoring Agent Behavior at Scale
modules.sub: Detecting anomalous agent credential use across distributed environments
modules.description: Learners configure logging and behavioral baselines for AI agent activity, identifying the signals — unusual API call patterns, off-hours credential use, unexpected resource access — that indicate compromise or misuse. They evaluate tooling options for aggregating and correlating non-human identity events across cloud, SaaS, and on-premises environments at scale. The module culminates in learners designing a monitoring playbook that ties detection alerts to response runbooks specific to agent identity incidents.

synopsis: AI agents are being granted real credentials, API keys, and system privileges — yet most identity governance frameworks were built for humans, leaving machine identities dangerously under-managed. This course teaches security and IAM professionals exactly how agent identities are exploited and how to close those gaps with modern controls.
rationale: Non-human identities now outnumber human identities in many enterprises, yet purpose-built governance for AI agents remains an almost entirely unaddressed blind spot in most IAM programs.
learning_outcome: Learners can audit an AI agent deployment for identity risks, apply zero-trust principles to non-human principals, and configure monitoring to detect anomalous agent credential use.
source_signals: Keeper Security: The identity security platform for humans, machines, and AI agents
Launch HN: Didit (YC W26) – Stripe for Identity Verification
Zero-Click Attacks: AI Agents and the Next Cybersecurity Challenge
